summary: DHH reflects on social media's role during the Russian invasion of Ukraine, arguing that despite its well-documented problems, it has proven uniquely powerful in humanizing the conflict and galvanizing Western support. He contrasts Ukraine's effective social media campaign—featuring direct, personal content from politicians and President Zelenskyy—with Russia's outdated, choreographed propaganda. He argues this media prowess has been instrumental in expediting Western sanctions, military aid, and public solidarity. He acknowledges the uneven empathy Europeans show toward Ukraine versus other conflicts, but frames this as basic human nature rather than something to intellectualize away. He closes with characteristic ambivalence, noting social media is simultaneously good and bad.
key_points: Ukraine's social media strategy—mixing personal moments with wartime updates—has been far more effective than Russia's old-school propaganda approach
The depth of public support activated through social media has directly expedited political action like SWIFT sanctions, German military spending, and airspace closures
The stronger European empathy toward Ukraine compared to Yemen or Syria reflects natural human psychology, not something to be ashamed of
Even a persistent critic of social media must acknowledge its unique capacity to give direct voice to people during a crisis
The long-term implications remain unknowable, and social media remains a mix of truth, fabrication, and fog of war
money_quotes: Being able to read, in real time, about members of the Ukrainian parliament taking up arms, mixed together with pictures of walking the dog, is a deeply humanizing experience.
It feels like a 20th century war propaganda machine firing mortars at a 21st century social media machine shooting lasers.
I think their invaders dramatically underestimated the power of that social media prowess to galvanize support from the West.
Being upset about the basic human graduation of empathy – that makes us care more about that which is closer and familiar – is an intellectualization.
As someone who rarely holds back any criticism of social media in general, and Twitter in particular, it's worth taking a moment to appreciate the difference it's making here and now.
Good, not good. Bad, not bad. Neither, both.
key_insight: Social media's capacity to humanize conflict through direct, unfiltered personal narratives can galvanize political action faster than any traditional propaganda, even as it remains a deeply flawed medium.
